reasoning: The flight attendant is explicitly described as fabricating a federal law to force the destruction of evidence regarding a safety hazard. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that flight crew authority extends to safety compliance, not the censorship of personal devices or the destruction of evidence, making this a clear case of exceeding delegated authority.

The security guard is clearly exceeding the scope of their delegated authority by inventing a rule (requiring a printed note) that contradicts the venue's official policy and endangers the user's life. No reasonable person would argue that a low-level gate agent has the legitimacy to confiscate insulin based on personal caprice rather than established rules.

Private security guards have no jurisdiction to enforce parking rules or immobilize vehicles on public city streets. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a private entity seizing property on public land constitutes an illegitimate exercise of authority.

A night concierge is an agent with limited delegated authority and cannot unilaterally invent rules that contradict the governing lease agreement. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a low-level employee has no right to override a contract between the tenant and the landlord or to invent non-existent bureaucratic forms.

The proctor's demand for full remote control to browse the user's hard drive clearly exceeds the scope of authority granted by the exam terms, which were limited to screen and webcam monitoring. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as a gross violation of privacy and security that falls outside the legitimate mandate of exam supervision.

The flight attendant is explicitly described as enforcing a personal preference that contradicts official airline policy and exceeds their professional authority. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a flight attendant does not have the legal right to order the destruction of personal data (photos of a child) merely because they appear in the background.

A private concert venue's security authority is reasonably understood to extend to physical safety (weapons) and intellectual property (recording devices). Demanding to search private digital communications (texts/apps) for 'illegal content' is a clear overreach of that delegated authority, effectively usurping a law enforcement function without a warrant.

A security guard fabricating a non-existent rule to exclude a visitor based on a personal grudge is a clear instance of an agent exceeding their delegated authority. No reasonable person would defend a guard's right to invent policy to settle personal scores, particularly when it prevents family from visiting a patient in the ICU.

A dean appointed by a military junta following a coup lacks legitimate authority to dictate academic curriculum, particularly when banning standard topics like 'western political theory'. A reasonable person would immediately recognize a military coup as an illegitimate source of governance and the censorship as an abuse of that usurped power.

A building superintendent demanding tenants install invasive location-tracking software on personal devices, without any basis in the signed lease agreement, is a clear excess of delegated authority. No reasonable person would accept that a property manager has the unilateral right to mandate digital surveillance of tenants outside of contractual terms.
